Proposed programme budget for 2027 – Programme planning

text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

2.1	The mandates of the Department for General Assembly and Conference Management derive from the relevant rules of procedure and resolutions of the principal organs of the United Nations, including General Assembly resolutions on the pattern of conferences (43/222, the most recent being 80/234), multilingualism (50/11, the most recent being 78/330) and the revitalization of the work of the General Assembly (73/341, the most recent being 79/327). The Department is responsible for:

(a)	The facilitation, through the provision of procedural and technical secretariat support and authoritative advice, of the orderly and effective conduct of the deliberations and follow-up actions of the General Assembly, its General, First, Special Political and Decolonization (Fourth), Second and Third Committees and various subsidiary organs, the Economic and Social Council and most of its subsidiary bodies, and special United Nations conferences;

(b)	Assistance in the revitalization efforts of the Assembly and other United Nations bodies, including through the substantive servicing of meetings of the Working Group on the Strengthening and Revitalizing of the Work of the General Assembly and the intergovernmental negotiations on the question of equitable representation on and increase in the membership of the Security Council and other matters relating to the Council;

(c)	Substantive and technical secretariat support to the Committee on Conferences and high-quality conference-servicing support, including multilingual meeting and documentation services, to all intergovernmental and expert bodies meeting at Headquarters and at the United Nations Offices at Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi, and other conferences and meetings held under the auspices of the United Nations at other locations, under shared responsibility with the Directors-General of the United Nations Offices at Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi, taking into account the principle of equal treatment to be applied to all official languages in each organ of the Organization;

(d)	Protocol services for the Secretary-General, the Deputy Secretary-General and visiting high-level dignitaries, as well as host country liaison services for Member States and Permanent Observers and the provision, upon request, of protocol services to the President of the General Assembly.

2.2	In accordance with General Assembly resolution 57/283 B, the Under-Secretary-General for General Assembly and Conference Management sets the policies, formulates standards and guidelines, manages resources under section 2 of the programme budget and oversees operations at Headquarters, while the Directors-General of the United Nations Offices at Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi are responsible for ensuring the implementation of those policies, standards and guidelines and for managing day-to-day operations at their duty stations. Furthermore, the Secretary-General has promulgated the establishment of a framework for delegation of authority (see ST/SGB/2019/2). The Secretary-General’s bulletin on the organization of the Department (ST/SGB/2021/3) reflects the intergovernmental mandates confirming the division of responsibilities among the Under-Secretary-General and the Directors-General at Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi for delivering conference services in a standardized and integrated manner.

2.3	In addition, pursuant to General Assembly resolution 69/250, the Under-Secretary-General for General Assembly and Conference Management is responsible for the Secretariat-wide coordination of multilingualism, a core value of the United Nations, and serves as system-wide lead entity on this matter in collaboration with the secretariat of the United Nations System Chief Executives Board for Coordination. With reference to Assembly resolution 78/330, the Office of the Coordinator for Multilingualism will continue to facilitate, from within existing capacity, a coordinated, consistent and coherent approach to multilingualism across the Secretariat.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

2.4	The 2027 programme plan is guided by the Department’s long-standing strategy of continuous modernization and improvement in all areas. The Department will continue to cultivate an organizational culture that advances agility, creativity, learning and adaptability and further develop cutting-edge skills in the areas of innovation, data analytics, digital transformation, strategic foresight and behavioural science that will enable it to harness new technological capabilities and further optimize its operations.

2.5	The Department will continue to invest, through its Global Innovation and Data Analytics Service and by utilizing available resources, in systems that enhance integrated global management and the efficiency, effectiveness, continuity, sustainability and accessibility of the services it provides, in line with the priorities outlined in the Secretary-General’s information and communications technology strategy. The Department will also continue to explore additional benefits that artificial intelligence, when used with adequate human oversight and appropriate quality control, could bring. The integrated global management of conference-servicing resources will be further enhanced by the central coordinating role of the new Evaluation, Analysis and Monitoring Service, which will strengthen these functions and harmonize them across the four duty stations.

2.6	The new, flexible job profiles for staff working in language and conference services introduced in 2026 will enable the Department to respond more agilely to its dynamic workload and changes brought about by evolving working methods. Cross-functional training, in addition to technical and subject-matter training, will continue to increase the versatility of staff and the agility of the Department as a whole.

2.7	The effective and efficient utilization of available resources and programme delivery will be ensured through clearly defined results-based performance indicators and benchmarks outlined under the respective subprogrammes in the present document. To manage surges in the demand of services, the Department will leverage temporary assistance to complement its in-house teams of interpreters, editors, translators, précis-writers, verbatim reporters and production editors. Competitive recruitment examinations for both core and temporary staff will be conducted as required.

2.8	The proactive analysis of client needs and client feedback, including through a global survey, will continue to guide the Department’s activities in developing its services in support of the work of the intergovernmental and expert bodies.
2.9	The Department will coordinate measures to enhance multilingualism in the Organization in accordance with the terms of reference of the Coordinator for Multilingualism (A/71/757, annex II).

2.10	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Department will continue to provide substantive and technical secretariat support and serve as the permanent Chair of the International Annual Meeting on Language Arrangements, Documentation and Publications. The Department will also promote and follow up on recommendations emanating from the common initiative for policy and technical solutions for artificial intelligence-powered language services, a far-reaching efficiency initiative of the High-level Committee on Management that the Department, starting in 2026, is co-leading with the International Civil Aviation Organization and the International Telecommunication Union.
2.11	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Information on changes to the calendar of conferences and meetings, as well as to the programmes of work of intergovernmental and expert bodies, is made available in a timely manner and in conformity with the applicable rules of procedure;
(b)	The number of non-calendar meetings does not exceed that of 2025;
(c)	Extraordinary meetings, including special and emergency special sessions, will be scheduled with sufficient notice;
(d)	Unforeseen high-priority documentation will be manageable;
(e)	Stakeholders will fulfil their responsibilities and obligations in accordance with established rules for the submission of documentation and meeting requests in a predictable manner;
(f)	Global supply chains enable the timely sourcing of high-quality goods, capacity and services.
2.12	The programme integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate.

2.13	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Department will continue to adjust and refine its systems, platforms, workflows and requirements for conference services, including seating arrangements, with a view to ensuring equal participation by delegates and participants with disabilities in the work of the United Nations.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

2.14	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects efforts of the programme to implement all mandates in a more efficient and effective manner and contains no substantive adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative.

2.15	The Department will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including the decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Legislative mandates
2.16	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides the mandates entrusted to the programme.

children.text: Evaluation activities
2.18	A global evaluation on workforce planning and capacity requirements for the future, conducted by the Department and completed in 2025, has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

2.19	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, the Department made informed decisions regarding workforce planning based on the information and data on workload, staffing levels, retirements and vacancies.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs
Objective

2.20	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure efficient and effective deliberation and decision-making processes of intergovernmental and relevant expert bodies and United Nations conferences.
Strategy
2.21	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide technical secretariat services to, and coordinate substantive support for, intergovernmental bodies, including the General Assembly, its General, First, Special Political and Decolonization (Fourth), Second and Third Committees and its various subsidiary and ad hoc bodies; the Economic and Social Council and its forums and subsidiary and ad hoc bodies; the Trusteeship Council; and conferences and other ad hoc and extraordinary meetings held under the auspices of the United Nations;
(b)	Provide procedural advice and substantive, analytical and historical information;

(c)	In response to requests from Member States, strengthen its core software tools, including e-deleGATE, which is available in the official languages of the United Nations and is able to capture interaction among various intergovernmental bodies, and which, along with existing e-deleGATE modules, the General Assembly, in its resolution 77/335, called upon its subsidiary organs to use, as much as possible.
2.22	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The smooth conduct of meetings of the intergovernmental bodies serviced;
(b)	Expanded availability and accessibility of secretariat support to the delegations;
(c)	Improved user experience for delegations on the e-deleGATE portal.
Programme performance in 2025
Increasing number of informal processes conducted by President of the General Assembly-appointed co-facilitators

2.23	As part of the technical secretariat services provided to the General Assembly, the subprogramme supports co-facilitators appointed by the President of the General Assembly in the informal consultations that they conduct with a view to reaching agreement on draft resolutions. The number of such processes has increased notably over the years. Given this increase and owing to liquidity constraints in 2025, the subprogramme was no longer able to provide in-person coverage for all such meetings; however, the subprogramme supported all the informal consultations towards their outcomes by providing procedural advice and organizational support.
2.24	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 2.I).
Figure 2.I
Performance measure: number of co-facilitation processes supported during sessions of the General Assembly (annual)
Planned results for 2027

The target was not met owing to liquidity constraints, which prevented the development of the centralized module.

children.text: Result 3: improved availability of e-deleGATE modules to subsidiary organs – Proposed programme plan for 2027

2.29	The General Assembly, in its resolutions 75/325 and 77/335, called upon its subsidiary organs to use, as much as possible, existing e-deleGATE modules. The subprogramme collaborates with secretariats of subsidiary organs in New York and away from Headquarters to facilitate their use of e-deleGATE.
Lessons learned and planned change

2.30	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the current architecture of e-deleGATE makes granting access to its modules to subsidiary organs a very time-consuming activity that involves multiple steps and staff from several units. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will redesign the platform to enable subsidiary organs, through their secretariats, to deploy e-deleGATE modules on a self-service basis through a user-friendly interface.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Planning and coordination of conference services
Objective

2.33	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure efficient, effective and multilingual deliberation and decision-making processes of intergovernmental and expert bodies and conferences serviced by the United Nations in New York, Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi.
Strategy
2.34	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Plan and manage meetings and documentation services and optimize capacity in a globally coordinated manner by leveraging technologies, including gMeets, gDoc and gData, and optimizing workflows;
(b)	Engage in regular consultations with Member States and substantive and technical secretariats to address conference-servicing needs in New York, Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi;
(c)	Provide technical and substantive support to the Committee on Conferences in New York;
(d)	Develop innovative processes and best practices to meet the evolving needs of global conferencing servicing.
2.35	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The addressing of Member States’ needs with responsive, timely, efficient and user-friendly conference services;
(b)	Smooth deliberations in the intergovernmental process across New York, Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi;
(c)	Well-established and mature standard operating procedures complemented by information technology systems applied to all services for Member States;
(d)	The timely and simultaneous issuance of parliamentary documents in all six official languages.
Programme performance in 2025
Increased number of meetings held away from Headquarters serviced

2.36	The subprogramme planned and coordinated 17 major conferences and meetings held away from Headquarters in 2025, compared with 13 in 2024 and 12 in 2023. The conferences coordinated away from Headquarters in 2025 were held in the following countries: Brazil, Canada, Costa Rica, Ethiopia, France, Germany, Morocco, Panama, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Spain, Thailand, Turkmenistan and Uruguay.

2.37	The planning and coordination teams at the four conference-servicing duty stations collaborated closely to ensure effective and efficient resource allocation and service delivery. Conference-servicing teams were strategically put together on the basis of the proximity rule of the integrated global management approach, in combination with other relevant considerations. Dedicated sections for each conference away from Headquarters were created in the Journal of the United Nations in all official languages. These included information on side events and special events that facilitated multi-stakeholder engagement.  The subprogramme also coordinated and facilitated many bilateral meetings among the Member States and other key stakeholders held on the margin of the conferences. The arrangement of such meetings was supported by the conference management systems developed and utilized by the subprogramme at all four duty stations, including the gMeets portal.
2.38	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 2.II).
Figure 2.II
Performance measure: number of conferences held away from Headquarters planned and coordinated by conference-servicing duty stations (annual)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: global gateway for servicing conferences and meetings – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

2.39	The subprogramme’s work contributed to clients, except those in Geneva, being able to request meeting services, obtain cost estimates and receive a single bill through the One-Stop Shop module of gMeets, which did not meet the planned target of all clients at all duty stations being able to do so. The target was not met because a full roll-out of One-Stop Shop in Geneva had to be postponed owing to liquidity constraints.
2.40	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 2.6).

children.text: Result 2: increased feedback from Member States through the global survey – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

2.41	The subprogramme did not meet the planned target of 500 responses to the global survey. The target was not met owing to technical difficulties resulting in data integrity issues, which made it impossible to determine which responses were valid, undermining the ability of the subprogramme to accurately report thereon.
2.42	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 2.III).
Figure 2.III
Performance measure: number of responses to the global survey (annual)
Abbreviation: N/A, not applicable.
Result 3: resilient operations ensured through cross-training in conference services – Proposed programme plan for 2027

2.43	Overall satisfaction by Member States with conference service has been consistently high (more than 85 per cent). To ensure continuity of high-quality services, notwithstanding reduced operational capacity, the subprogramme conducted an analysis of business processes in the planning and coordination of conference services.
Lessons learned and planned change

2.44	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, when resources were limited, having a versatile workforce capable of performing several functions augmented capacity for mandate delivery and allowed for robust service continuity. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will further streamline business processes, revise job profiles and expand cross-training in the form of hands-on experience in critical operational tasks to support the end-to-end sustainability and quality of conference services by having documentation, meetings and conferences planned and coordinated by a versatile workforce capable of performing multiple functions. These measures will foster adaptability and resilience, ensuring that more staff can seamlessly switch between roles when needed, such that the subprogramme can continue to contribute to the high level of satisfaction by Member States with conference services.
2.45	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure2.IV).
Figure 2.IV
Performance measure: overall satisfaction by Member States with conference services, in line with the global survey (annual)

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Documentation services
Objective

2.47	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure efficient and effective multilingual deliberation and decision-making processes of intergovernmental and expert bodies and conferences serviced by the United Nations in New York, Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi.
Strategy
2.48	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Deliver multilingual documentation services, including translation, editing, précis-writing, report-writing, desktop publishing and correspondence services, while improving cost-effectiveness by leveraging technologies, optimizing workflows, matching capacity with workload and developing staff skills through training;

(b)	Promote the versatility of language professionals with regard to processing highly complex legal and technical documents and performing other linguistic functions by providing continuous subject-matter training, including by leading projects on knowledge management and training and by organizing briefings with substantive secretariats;

(c)	Explore new technological solutions and develop and maintain existing systems, including gText and gDoc, to ensure the effective planning and processing of multilingual documentation within established time frames and to support linguists in the fulfilment of the mandate on quality;
(d)	Strengthen quality assurance mechanisms and manage the quality of internally and externally processed documents by applying rigorous quality-control standards;
(e)	Maintain and enhance the UNTERM portal;
(f)	Convert categories of documents into machine-readable format to develop further information services that will leverage the wealth of the Organization’s knowledge;
(g)	Hold remote competitive examinations and build rosters of suitably qualified language professionals for employment as staff or on a contractual basis, as needed;
(h)	Undertake targeted outreach to universities in line with recruitment needs.
2.49	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The timely availability of high-quality documents, issued simultaneously in the official languages, in compliance with existing mandates;
(b)	The consistent use of official terminology in the official languages;
(c)	Improved access to knowledge resources through the provision of machine-readable documents;
(d)	Adequate linguistic capacity to sustain the delivery of high-quality and timely multilingual documentation services.
Programme performance in 2025
Timely issuance of documentation sustained, notwithstanding constraints

2.50	The subprogramme faced exceptional challenges in 2025. Not only did the liquidity constraints prevent it from employing external capacity to supplement internal teams during workload surges, unexpected additional workload, such as the extra 340,000 words submitted in New York in relation to the revised estimates relating to the proposed programme budget for 2026 and support account for peacekeeping operations for the 2025/26 period and the 90,000 words associated with the new Intergovernmental Science-Policy Panel on Chemicals, Waste and Pollution established in Nairobi, significantly increased the pressure on documentation services at all duty stations.

2.51	The subprogramme responded by making every effort to maximize internal capacity, working closely with planning and coordination colleagues to determine processing priorities and proactively with author departments and submitting entities to clarify substantive or terminology matters, thus saving processing time. In Vienna, such efforts enabled six resolutions and seven decisions at the sixty-eighth session of the Commission on Narcotic Drugs to be delivered in time for the votes. Workload was also shared to the extent possible. The subprogramme in Geneva, for example, translated the pre-session documentation for the International Law Commission previously translated in New York.

2.52	These actions, together with the flexibility and professionalism of staff, ensured that, notwithstanding the liquidity constraints, Member States continued to receive high-quality documentation in a timely manner, with global timely issuance being 93 per cent in 2025, compared with 92 per cent in 2023 and 2024 (see figure 2.VI).
2.53	Progress towards the objective, further detailed at the duty station level, is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 2.V).
Figure 2.V
Performance measure: percentage of parliamentary documentation issued in a timely manner (annual)
Planned results for 2027

children.text: Result 2: timely availability of documentation for meetings – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

2.56	The subprogramme’s work contributed to a global timely issuance rate of 93 per cent of parliamentary documentation, which did not meet the planned target of 100 per cent. The target was not met because the subprogramme operated with reduced capacity as a result of the liquidity constraints. However, the renegotiation of deadlines and the rescheduling of parliamentary documents meant that all documents were delivered ahead of the relevant meetings, although not six weeks in advance.
2.57	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 2.VI).
Figure 2.VI
Performance measure: global timely issuance of parliamentary documentation (annual)
(Percentage)
Result 3: equal treatment of official languages upheld – Proposed programme plan for 2027

2.58	The simultaneous issuance of high-quality parliamentary documentation in the official languages is essential to support the intergovernmental process and uphold the Department’s multilingualism mandate. Given the challenges facing the language services, such as capacity imbalances stemming from uneven retirements that are compounded by the Organization’s financial situation, which limits the subprogramme’s ability to hire external capacity, simultaneous issuance has become increasingly difficult. In this regard, the Department has consistently leveraged new technologies, including neural machine translation, to increase efficiency while investing in staff and providing them with the necessary skills and tools, given that technologies in and of themselves cannot ensure the level of accuracy required of parliamentary documentation. The subprogramme therefore explored opportunities offered by cross-functional teams, contributing to the revision of generic job profiles, and implemented knowledge-sharing platforms, improved workload-sharing processes and adopted new internal working methods across the four duty stations. This work has helped to sustain quality and productivity, resolve bottlenecks during peak periods and ensure the simultaneous issuance of high-quality documentation.
Lessons learned and planned change

2.59	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, while technology improved efficiency, human expertise continued to be needed to ensure accuracy, completeness and correct terminology use. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will continue to implement upskilling programmes to enhance the ability to understand, use and evaluate artificial intelligence technologies, provide continuous learning opportunities and support operational and collaborative resilience in a context of continuous change, development and modernization.
2.60	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 2.VII).
Figure 2.VII
Performance measure: simultaneous issuance of parliamentary documentation in the six official languages (annual)
(Percentage)

children.text: a	Excludes editorial corrections generated by parallel processing, which is necessary to ensure timeliness.
Subprogramme 4 – Meetings and publishing services
Objective

2.62	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure efficient and effective multilingual deliberation and decision-making processes of intergovernmental and expert bodies and conferences serviced by the United Nations in New York, Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi.
Strategy
2.63	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide multilingual interpretation, meeting support and publishing services for conferences and meetings held at the four conference-servicing duty stations and those held away from Headquarters;
(b)	Provide multilingual verbatim reporting services at Headquarters in New York;

(c)	Utilize data-driven methodologies to project, plan and obtain the staff and temporary workforce capacity necessary to sustain peak periods of demand and for meetings held away from Headquarters, including through global innovation projects in areas related to interpretation, such as the interpreters assignment programme (eAPG);
(d)	Promote the versatility of language professionals to perform other linguistic functions, by providing training and coaching;
(e)	Develop and implement good practices relating to conference support and the sustainability and accessibility of meetings and publishing services.
2.64	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Effective matching of servicing capacity with expected increases in meeting demand during peak periods;
(b)	Enhanced accessibility and continued sustainability of publishing services and of conference support services for meetings participants.
Programme performance in 2025
Quality maintained and efficiencies generated for meeting organizers through integrated global management of interpretation resources

2.65	The application of the proximity rule, whereby, to the extent possible, meeting services, including interpretation, are provided by the closest duty station to reduce the associated travel costs, plays a key role in reducing the outlays associated with meetings and conferences held away from Headquarters. The notional savings for the organizers of such events were $839,244 in 2023 and $1,057,063 in 2024, of which $705,426 and $1,013,393, respectively, corresponded to interpretation.

2.66	Effective integrated global planning and management of interpretation capacity at the four conference-servicing duty stations, in coordination with subprogramme 2, enabled the subprogramme to continue to support the application of the proximity rule. Staff also supported operational continuity by scheduling leave predominantly during off-peak periods, thereby helping to maintain adequate coverage during periods of high demand. Overall, those collective efforts enabled the subprogramme to sustain high-quality interpretation services across a demanding and geographically diverse meeting calendar and generate notional savings associated with interpretation of $754,200 in 2025.
2.67	In this way, the subprogramme both ensured the highest-quality interpretation and contributed significantly to the $839,514 of total notional savings generated for meeting organizers in 2025.
2.68	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 2.VIII).
Figure 2.VIII

Performance measure: notional savings generated for organizers of meetings held away from Headquarters under the proximity rule of integrated global management for the assignment of interpretation resources (annual)
(United States dollars)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: increased interpretation services available to meet the growing demand of Member States for meetings with interpretation – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

2.69	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 5,828 meetings being held with interpretation, which did not meet the planned target of 7,236. The target was not met in part owing to the liquidity constraints in 2025, which prevented the subprogramme from hiring temporary external capacity.
2.70	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 2.IX).
Figure 2.IX

children.text: Result 3: meeting coverage sustained by leveraging in-house interpretation planning technology – Proposed programme plan for 2027

2.73	Optimal planning and deployment of in-house and external interpretation capacity is crucial to sustain meeting coverage, especially during periods of peak demand. To enable proactive workforce management and respond more agilely to the Organization’s needs, the subprogramme has been developing and improving eAPG, the Department’s in-house tool for managing both interpretation capacity and assignments. In 2025, to allow for global management of interpretation resources and forecasting of resource requirements in real time, eAPG capabilities were enhanced with the integration of time and attendance data, enabling full coverage of all mandated meetings with interpretation and the servicing of meetings of major groups using in-house resources, when such resources were available.
Lessons learned and planned change

2.74	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the capacity management features of the eAPG application remained limited. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will further enhance eAPG through the introduction of new mechanisms for the global management of interpreters’ profiles, which display information such as the language combinations and availability of freelancers, as well as expanded modules for resource planning, to enable more cost-effective responses to medium-term and last-minute requests for meetings and needs to replace interpreters.

Stocktaking of eAPG functionalities to identify potential improvements for better capacity management
Improved system security through the discontinuation of legacy tools and the introduction of the Azure Active Directory login
More refined capacity analysis tools implemented, leading to optimized use of in-house capacity

The pooling of in-house capacity across duty stations and units as part of integrated global management is better supported through new mechanisms for managing global profiles of staff and freelance interpreters in eAPG
Optimization of temporary assistance facilitated through integration of the global language register into eAPG and a feature for tentative recruitment forecasting
